senses.definition: the first card played by a player at the beginning of a trick, which sets the suit for that trick and determines the order in which other players must play their cards
(bridge) the process by which players bid on the right to name the trump suit and the number of tricks they expect to take in a particular hand
(bridge) the process by which players communicate information about their hand to their partner and determine the contract for the hand
(contract bridge) an agreement that determines the trump suit and the number of tricks a team must win to score points
the player who has won the auction and has the right to play the contract
the partner of the declarer in a bridge game, whose hand is displayed face-up on the table for all players to see after the opening lead

examples.x: The dummy's cards are revealed after the first card is played in a bridge game.
As the declarer's partner, the dummy can only watch as the declarer plays their hand.
The dummy's cards are an essential part of the strategy in bridge.
The declarer's success often depends on how well they use the dummy's hand.
In the game, the dummy provides an advantage by allowing the declarer to plan their moves based on the visible cards.

senses.definition: a technique of attempting to win a trick with a lower card than an opponent's higher card in a particular suit
any of the top four cards (ace, king, queen, and jack) in a particular suit that are considered to be the strongest cards in that suit
a bidding convention used by the responder to show a five-card or longer major suit and to transfer the bid to the next higher ranking suit, usually at the 2-level, forcing the opener to bid the suit

examples.x: By making a Jacoby transfer, I helped my partner become the declarer with the suit I was strongest in.
During the game, I was able to signal to my partner using a Jacoby transfer, allowing them to play the hand with my best suit.
Instead of bidding hearts directly, I made a Jacoby transfer, so my partner could take control of the suit.
We won the hand thanks to a successful Jacoby transfer, which allowed my partner to play the suit they were more comfortable with.

senses.definition: (bridge) either the hearts or spades suits, which are considered stronger than the minor suits (diamonds and clubs) because they have more high-ranking cards

examples.x: The player opened with a bid in hearts, which is a major suit.
In the auction, the team decided to bid a major suit to gain more points.
He was holding several high cards in a major suit, making his hand very powerful.
If you have a good hand in a major suit, you might consider making a strong bid.
The contract was set in a major suit, so the declarer had to win more tricks.

senses.definition: (bridge) either the diamonds or clubs suits, which are considered weaker than the major suits (hearts and spades) because they have fewer high-ranking cards

examples.x: The player decided to bid on a minor suit, hoping to win enough tricks despite the weaker hand.
In the first round, I had more clubs than any other suit, so I chose to bid the minor suit.
The partnership was able to win with a minor suit, though it required careful strategy.
He played the minor suit hoping his opponent would not have enough high cards to win the trick.
I had three diamonds, so I decided to focus on the minor suit for the game.

senses.definition: a bid made by the declarer that specifies no trump suit and indicates that the declarer intends to win tricks using only the strength and distribution of the cards, without relying on any particular suit

examples.x: In the final round, the declarer called no-trump, making it harder for the opponents to use their suits effectively.
With no-trump, the game became more challenging, as the usual advantage of a trump suit was removed.
The strategy behind bidding no-trump is to signal that you have a strong hand without a specific suit to dominate.
In no-trump games, it is often harder to control the play, since no suit has a special advantage.

senses.definition: a person we do a particular activity with, such as playing a game

examples.x: Alex's tennis partner is really skilled, and they make a great team.
In the cooking class, each student has a different partner every week.
Mark and Lisa are practice partners for learning a new musical instrument.
Paul and Sarah decided to become workout partners at the gym to motivate each other.
Sarah found a dance partner to participate in the upcoming competition.

senses.definition: the points or score awarded to the defending side when they successfully prevent the declarer from making their bid
the partner of the opening bidder who makes the first bid after the opening bid and provides additional information about their hand to the opening bidder
a series of games in which one partnership must win two games, with the total score determining the winner

examples.x: The bridge club was thrilled when they finally won the rubber after an intense match.
They played a rubber of three games to determine the ultimate winner.
After losing the first game, the team strategized carefully to win the rubber.
The rubber consisted of three deals, with each team aiming for the highest score.
The excitement grew as the final game of the rubber approached its conclusion.

senses.definition: a bidding system used by the responder to ask the opener if they have a four-card major suit, typically either hearts or spades, after an opening bid of 1NT

examples.x: The Stayman convention helps us avoid a no-trump contract if we can find a good major suit fit.
By using the Stayman convention, the responder can ask the opener if they have a four-card major suit.
They decided to use the Stayman convention to increase their chances of finding a stronger suit.

senses.definition: a situation where a player has no cards in a particular suit, which means they cannot follow suit in that suit and must play a card from another suit instead
a suit in which a player holds a large number of cards, typically at least five or more, which can be an advantage in playing and winning tricks in that suit
a suit in which a player holds a small number of cards, typically fewer than three, which can be a disadvantage in playing and winning tricks in that suit

examples.x: She played her short suit early in the hand to force the opponents to waste their high cards.
In bridge, a short suit is usually a sign that you may need to rely on your partner to cover that suit.
He had a short suit in diamonds, so he focused on strengthening his hearts and spades.
A short suit often means fewer opportunities to win tricks in that suit.

senses.definition: a contract bid in which the declarer aims to win all thirteen tricks, that is, to take all the remaining tricks after the opening lead
a contract bid in which the declarer aims to win all thirteen tricks, that is, to take all the remaining tricks after the opening lead, using the trump suit as the primary weapon
a unit of value, especially monetary value
(in bridge, whist, or other similar card games) to successfully fulfill their contract by taking the required number of tricks

examples.x: Despite challenging circumstances, the skilled player managed to make the contract by taking all the necessary tricks.
The strategic bidding allowed the team to make their slam contract and secure a substantial point advantage.
The declarer's clever play enabled them to make the game contract, earning valuable points for their team.
The precise card play helped the partnership make the contract, outsmarting the opposing defenders.
Despite the opponents' attempts to thwart their plans, the declarer still managed to make the vulnerable game contract.

senses.definition: the number of tricks won by the declarer that are over and above the number of tricks required to fulfill their contract

examples.x: The declarer needed to win four odd tricks to fulfill their contract of four hearts.
After winning eight tricks, the team counted two as odd tricks for their bid.
The defenders tried to stop the declarer from winning any odd tricks to set their contract.
The contract required five odd tricks, so the declarer needed to win 11 tricks in total.

senses.definition: a trick won by the declarer in excess of the number of tricks required to fulfill their contract

examples.x: The declarer made their contract and even earned an overtrick by winning an extra trick.
Scoring an overtrick in duplicate bridge can make a big difference in competitive rankings.
The defenders tried hard to stop the declarer, but they still ended up giving away an overtrick.
In no-trump contracts, overtricks can add valuable points to the final score.
The declarer was confident in their play and aimed for an overtrick to boost their score.

senses.definition: the number of tricks that the declarer fails to take in attempting to fulfill their contract, and they can result in penalties for the declarer's side

examples.x: A single undertrick might not seem costly, but in duplicate bridge, it can make a big difference in the rankings.
The declarer's risky bid resulted in three undertricks, giving the opponents a large penalty.
The declarer fell short of their contract by two tricks, so the defenders scored penalties for each undertrick.

senses.definition: a penalty or negative score incurred when a player exceeds a certain number of tricks or points that they initially bid or predicted to win during the game
a series of cards with decreasing numbers, all of the same suit, that comes after the main set of cards

examples.x: The player was excited to see a back run of hearts in their hand, which could lead to winning several tricks.
In the middle of the game, she noticed she had a back run in diamonds, which she hoped to use to her advantage.
The back run of clubs was perfect for his strategy, giving him the ability to take multiple tricks.
If you have a back run in your hand, it can be a good idea to focus on that suit to maximize your chances of winning.

senses.definition: a scoring system used in duplicate bridge tournaments that assigns points based on the margin of victory for each deal, with one IMP typically awarded for every 20 points scored above the opponents' result
to fail to follow suit when a player should have, violating the rules of the card game being played

examples.x: He accidentally revoked by playing a spade instead of following the suit.
She realized she had revoked after the round had already ended.
The referee reviewed the game and confirmed the player had revoked.
If you revoke in bridge, the opposing team gains a penalty.
He revoked by playing the wrong card, unaware that he still had one in the required suit.

senses.definition: in card games, particularly in games like Spades, refers to the act of bidding zero tricks and attempting to win no tricks during a round
to play a trump card in a trick-taking game when unable to follow suit, usually with the intention of winning the trick or avoiding losing a higher-value card
to play a card of a suit that outranks the current suit, usually used in trick-taking card games to win a trick and gain an advantage over other players
